one_line_takeaway: CoRM-RAG reranks RAG evidence by a distilled 'robustness-under-simulated-cognitive-bias' score instead of semantic similarity, cutting the clean-to-biased accuracy gap on a synthetic Biased-NQ benchmark from 6.3 (Contriever) to 1.3 while running at cross-encoder-level latency (~215ms vs. ~20s for LLM reranking) -- a hallucination/sycophancy-mitigation method for naive-user bias, not an adversarial-attack defense.
key_finding: On Biased-NQ (Table 1), CoRM-RAG reaches 52.6% accuracy vs. Contriever 39.5%, BM25 28.0%, standard Cross-Encoder 40.9%, PA-CE 48.0%, LLM-Rerank (GPT-4o) 51.0%, Self-RAG 46.5%, and CalibRAG 48.0%; CoRM-RAG's clean-to-biased Robustness Gap is 1.3 (lowest of all methods) vs. e.g. 6.3 for Contriever and PA-CE, and 3.5 for LLM-Rerank. Latency is ~215ms for CoRM-RAG vs. ~20s for LLM-Rerank (paper states ~93x faster) and 2200ms for CalibRAG. Cross-generator transfer: with Llama-3-8B as generator, CoRM-RAG scores 46.5% vs. Cross-Encoder 42.8% on Biased-NQ (+3.7pp); with GPT-4o as generator, 53.2% vs. 46.9% (Gap drops from 6.9 to 1.9). Ablating PA-CE vs. CoRM-RAG isolates the listwise-vs-pointwise objective: +4.6pp gain (52.6% vs 48.0%) attributed to the listwise ranking loss rather than the adversarial training data alone. Risk-coverage analysis (Figure 3): abstaining on the bottom 20% lowest-confidence queries raises selective accuracy from 52.6% to 62.0%; reducing coverage to 50% raises it to 78.0%. Retrieval-quality analysis on a 2,728-query subset with at least one gold-bearing passage in the top-100 pool: Recall@1 improves from 36.5% (Cross-Encoder) to 48.3% (CoRM-RAG), a +11.8pp gain; Recall@5 improves from 66.8% to 77.8%. Per-query paired rank comparison: CoRM-RAG ranks the gold passage strictly higher than the Cross-Encoder for 45.7% of queries vs. 27.1% where the Cross-Encoder wins (27.2%/27.3% ties, net +18.6pp). Perturbation-type lesion study (Figure 5): removing False-Premise (Type I) training drops Type I test accuracy 50.2% -> 45.4% (-4.8pp); removing Confirmation-Bias (Type II) training drops Type II accuracy 53.5% -> 45.2% (-8.3pp); removing Distraction (Type III) training drops Type III accuracy 54.1% -> 46.5% (-7.6pp), with each ablation's effect largely confined to its own perturbation type (diagonal dominance), evidencing the three bias types require distinct learned signals. Hyperparameter sensitivity: accuracy gains from perturbation depth K diminish beyond K=5; even K=1 (data-matched to PA-CE) beats PA-CE, isolating the algorithmic (listwise) benefit; retrieval depth M expanding from 10 to 100 yields +8.7pp accuracy ('Buried Truth' effect, since robust corrective documents often rank low under plain semantic similarity), while the standard Cross-Encoder does not benefit from deeper retrieval.
core_contribution: The paper introduces CoRM-RAG (Counterfactual Risk Minimization for RAG), a retrieval framework that reranks candidate documents by predicted 'robustness utility' -- the probability that a document keeps an LLM's decision correct under simulated user cognitive biases -- rather than by semantic relevance alone. Concretely: (1) a Cognitive Perturbation Protocol that uses an Adversary LLM (Qwen3-32B for training, GPT-4o for test) to rewrite queries into three bias types -- Type I False-Premise Rewriting, Type II Confirmation-Bias Rewriting, Type III Topical Distraction; (2) a teacher-student distillation pipeline where a Teacher LLM (Qwen-3-14B) evaluates each candidate document's ability to sustain the correct answer across K perturbed queries, producing a soft robustness label s in {0,1/K,...,1}; (3) a lightweight Evidence Critic (DeBERTa-v3-large cross-encoder) trained with a hybrid listwise ranking loss (L_rank, temperature-scaled softmax cross-entropy over per-perturbation candidate groups) plus a pointwise calibration loss (L_conf, binary cross-entropy against the soft label); and (4) a Dynamic Robust Context inference procedure (Algorithm 1) that gates every candidate against a safety threshold gamma and abstains outright if even the top-ranked document falls below it.
threat_model: Not a security threat model. The 'adversary' in this paper is not a malicious attacker crafting inputs to compromise a system -- it is an LLM used as a tool to synthetically simulate a naive user's own cognitive biases (false premises, confirmation bias, topical distraction) baked sincerely into a query, explicitly excluding tell-tale hedging language so perturbations read as genuine mistaken beliefs. There is no adversarial actor trying to poison the corpus, exfiltrate data, jailbreak the model, or otherwise attack the system; the failure mode addressed is ordinary hallucination/sycophancy induced by a biased end user, not an attack. Framing this as an adversarial-robustness or security paper would be inaccurate.
limitations: The paper's own stated caveats and observable gaps: (1) On TruthfulQA, where misconceptions are baked into pretraining data rather than injected via explicit query context, the paper states CoRM-RAG's margin over baselines is 'narrower' than on Biased-NQ -- no exact TruthfulQA numbers beyond the Table 1 row (47.0% vs. baselines 34.9-46.5%) are broken out or discussed further. (2) Figure 3's risk-coverage curve shows 'jagged fluctuations in the low-coverage region (<10%)... revealing that even among the highest-confidence predictions, rare fatal hallucinations persist,' i.e., calibration is imperfect at the extreme high-confidence end. (3) Ground-truth accuracy judging relies on GPT-5 as an automatic judge rather than human annotation, and the correctness criterion for False-Premise queries (must state the true fact rather than the presupposed false one) is defined by the same paper's authors. (4) Robustness Utility is defined and estimated with respect to only the three specific perturbation types the authors designed (False Premise, Confirmation Bias, Topical Distraction); generalization to unseen bias categories is untested. (5) The Evidence Critic and Cognitive Perturbation Protocol were built and evaluated exclusively on NQ-derived, English, single-hop factoid QA; the Conclusion explicitly flags multi-hop reasoning and personalized/adaptive perturbation generation as future work, i.e., unaddressed by this paper. (6) All perturbation generation and Teacher/Critic supervision depend on other LLMs (Qwen3-32B, Qwen-3-14B, GPT-4o) as ground truth for what counts as 'robust,' so systematic biases or blind spots in those models could propagate into the Critic's notion of robustness -- this is not discussed by the authors.

datasets_used: Biased-NQ - self-constructed challenge set of 3,610 NQ test queries, balanced across False Premise / Confirmation Bias / Distraction perturbation types, test perturbations generated by GPT-4o (train perturbations by Qwen-3-32B to avoid leakage)
quantitative_results: Biased-NQ accuracy: CoRM-RAG 52.6% vs. Contriever 39.5%, BM25 28.0%, Cross-Encoder(MS MARCO) 40.9%, PA-CE 48.0%, LLM-Rerank(GPT-4o) 51.0%, Self-RAG 46.5%, CalibRAG 48.0%
Robustness Gap (Acc_clean - Acc_biased, lower better): CoRM-RAG 1.3 vs. Contriever 6.3, PA-CE 6.3, Cross-Encoder 5.2, LLM-Rerank 3.5, Self-RAG 3.1, CalibRAG 3.8, BM25 4.4
Latency: CoRM-RAG ~215ms vs. LLM-Rerank(GPT-4o) ~20s (paper states ~93x faster), CalibRAG 2200ms, Self-RAG 850ms, Contriever 25ms, BM25 <10ms
Cross-generator transfer on Biased-NQ: Llama-3-8B generator, CoRM-RAG 46.5% vs. Cross-Encoder 42.8% (+3.7pp); GPT-4o generator, CoRM-RAG 53.2% vs. Cross-Encoder 46.9%, gap 1.9 vs. 6.9
Recall@1 on gold-bearing Biased-NQ subset (n=2,728): CoRM-RAG 48.3% vs. Cross-Encoder 36.5% (+11.8pp); Recall@5: 77.8% vs. 66.8%
Per-query paired rank comparison: CoRM-RAG ranks gold passage higher in 45.7% of queries vs. Cross-Encoder wins 27.1%, ties 27.2-27.3% (net +18.6pp)
Risk-coverage: abstaining bottom 20% raises selective accuracy 52.6% -> 62.0%; 50% coverage raises it to 78.0%

Ablation on perturbation types: removing Type I training drops Type I accuracy 50.2% -> 45.4% (-4.8pp); removing Type II drops Type II accuracy 53.5% -> 45.2% (-8.3pp); removing Type III drops Type III accuracy 54.1% -> 46.5% (-7.6pp)
Retrieval depth M ablation: expanding M from 10 to 100 yields +8.7% accuracy for CoRM-RAG
baselines_compared: PA-CE (Perturbation-Augmented Cross-Encoder, pointwise BCE on same adversarial data)
